metadata.title: Why You Should Write the Test Before You Write the Prompt
metadata.purpose: Fluency and correctness are independent. Writing evaluation criteria before generating output forces you to articulate what success means — the information the prompt actually required.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: INK = criteria present / correct output; CRIMSON = criteria missing / failed output
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO rubric research methodology, NO extended test-driven development history, NO four-criteria taxonomy deep-dive, NO AI evaluation benchmark literature. Mechanism only: fluency and correctness are independent; criteria before generation forces explicit success specification.

signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. The proposal was polished, well-structured, and professionally written. The foundation rejected it. Not for quality — for content it never had, because no one had written down what success meant before generating it.

card.copy: Why you should write the test before you write the prompt
card.sub: output quality gives no signal about output correctness

beats.narration_text: Nadia is writing a federal grant proposal for her nonprofit's tutoring program. She prompts Claude: write a funding proposal. She edits the polished result for an hour, impressed by the clarity. She submits it.

beats.narration_text: The foundation scores it: clarity 9 out of 10. Equity lens: 4 out of 10. Measurable outcomes: 3 out of 10. None of those criteria appeared in her prompt. Why did output quality give no signal about output correctness?

card.copy: Why did output quality give no signal about output correctness?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The foundation scores it: clarity 9 out of 10. Equity lens: 4 out of 10. Measurable outcomes: 3 out of 10. None of those criteria appeared in her prompt. Why di

beats.narration_text: Fluency and correctness are independent. An output can be clear, complete-looking, and well-structured — and still fail every criterion the task actually required. Claude produces fluent text optimized for what it has. If success criteria aren't in the prompt, Claude has no target.
The reviewer's rubric existed. The funder always evaluates equity lens and measurable outcomes. Nadia knew this at some level. But she had not written it down — and so it was never in the prompt.

The mechanism is simple. Claude's output is optimized against whatever criteria it has. Give it no criteria — it optimizes for fluency, generic completeness, and the shape of similar documents. Those are defaults. Not yours.

Writing criteria before generating forces you to articulate what success means. That articulation is the information the prompt actually required. The criteria don't just improve the output — they reveal requirements you hadn't consciously stated.
Criteria also give you a review checklist. Instead of reading for general impressiveness — which is how automation bias traps you — you run five specific checks.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Criteria also give you a review checklist. Instead of reading for general impressiveness — which is how automation bias traps you — you run five specific checks

beats.narration_text: Nadia rewrites: the output must address equity explicitly, name three measurable outcomes, and include a logic model. Now Claude has targets. Now Nadia has a checklist. The proposal that follows meets all three.

The act of writing criteria before prompting is how you discover what you actually need. Grant writers who write acceptance criteria learn something about what the funder requires. Analysts who write failure criteria learn something about what they won't accept.

Project manager Li asks Claude for a status report. She gets 800 polished words. Her director asks: did it cover risk items? She checks — the report is thorough, organized, and doesn't mention the three flagged risks she was tracking. They were never in the prompt.

Li adds three criteria: the report must name each flagged risk, state its current status, and end with one recommended action per risk. The next output: 800 words covering exactly those. Polished and correct this time — because she specified what correct meant.

Before writing any prompt, ask two questions: what does good look like? And what would make this wrong regardless of how polished it is? Write both down. Those answers are the criteria — and the criteria are the actual specification.
Fluency and correctness are independent. Write what good looks like before you generate — and correctness has a target.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Fluency and correctness are independent. Write what good looks like before you generate — and correctness has a target.
